_provenance.note: P-256 ECDSA signatures are NOT byte-identical across upstream runtimes when the impl uses random-k (nodecrypto.ts, webcrypto.ts). This C# port mirrors upstream's purejs runtime which uses RFC 6979 deterministic-k AND low-S (noble v1.3.0 default), so the C# signature bytes ARE reproducible against an upstream purejs runtime. All three upstream runtimes produce signatures that verify under the same public key + SHA-256(content), so end-to-end Turnkey API calls work regardless of runtime.
_provenance.byte_level_assertion_strategy: Exact Node-generated byte snapshots would require running the upstream npm package with its full node_modules; the tarball ships only built JS + declarations. Instead, ApiKeyStamperTests asserts wire-format equivalence by: (1) reproducing the upstream stamp-test.ts shape (decoded JSON key order = ['publicKey', 'scheme', 'signature']; scheme constant; publicKey equality); (2) crypto-verifying the DER ECDSA signature against SHA-256(content) under the upstream fixture public key; (3) asserting RFC 6979 determinism (same content -> same signature); (4) asserting low-S (s in (0, n/2]). This combination is sufficient to guarantee the stamp is accepted by Turnkey's backend, which is the wire-format goal.

stampShape.stamp_test_assertion: Decode base64url -> JSON.parse; keys equal ['publicKey', 'scheme', 'signature']; publicKey == fixture.publicKey; scheme == SIGNATURE_SCHEME_TK_API_P256; signature verifies as DER ECDSA against SHA-256(message) under the fixture public key.
